news_summary: Advancements in quantum-secure technology through IRIS² promise enhanced cybersecurity and a safer digital future.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans le domaine des technologies quantiques sécurisées grâce à IRIS² laissent entrevoir une cybersécurité renforcée et un avenir numérique plus sûr.
news_summary_es: Los avances en tecnología cuántica segura gracias a IRIS² prometen una mayor ciberseguridad y un futuro digital más seguro.

articles.title: OBSERVER: A quantum secure future with IRIS²
articles.summary:
OBSERVER: A quantum secure future with IRIS² 
sonia
Thu, 16/04/2026 - 13:26

As cyber threats evolve and quantum computing advances towards practical applications, traditional encryption methods face more complex challenges. The Infrastructure for Resilience, Interconnectivity and Security by Satellite (IRIS²) constellation represents Europe's ambitious response to this emerging reality, positioning the European Union to integrate cutting-edge quantum security technologies to ensuring secure communications across Europe and Africa. IRIS² will become a multi-orbit satellite constellation for governmental and commercial communications, with an architecture creating opportunities for future quantum security implementations including quantum key distribution and post-quantum cryptography. In this Observer, we explore how quantum technologies could transform satellite communications security, examine the potential for quantum capabilities within IRIS², and assess what this means for Europe's digital sovereignty in the quantum age. 
 
Quantum computers differ from today’s classical machines. Instead of processing information in binary, as 0s or 1s, they use quantum bits, or qubits, which can exist in overlapping states and be linked together so their states influence one another. This gives quantum computers the ability to explore many possible solutions to problems in parallel, enabling them to run special algorithms designed for quantum systems to solve the mathematical problems underlying many current encryption methods. The potential ease and speed with which quantum computers could crack encryption poses a fundamental threat to cybersecurity infrastructure. Quantum computers already exist as experimental systems, but once cryptanalytically relevant machines become available, they will be capable of breaking the mathematical foundations underlying today’s encryption that protects everything from online banking to government communications.  
This looming threat, often referred to as Years to Quantum or “Y2Q”, has prompted governments and organisations worldwide to prepare for a post-quantum world. In order to anticipate risks and exploit growing opportunities, the European Commission announced its Quantum Europe Strategy in July 2025, recognising this challenge as a matter of strategic importance. The strategy marked Space and Dual-Use (the capacity of quantum technologies to serve both civilian and security/defence ends) technologies as a crucial area, in which IRIS² and the European Quantum Communication Infrastructure (EuroQCI) initiative present tremendous opportunities to leverage quantum technologies in the space domain. 
 
IRIS² and the quantum security challenge 
IRIS², Europe's sovereign multi-orbit constellation initiative, is being designed with quantum-ready security in mind. The European Union’s newest flagship space component, is led by the European Commission under a concession contract with the SpaceRISE consortium, working with trusted partners such as the European Space Agency (ESA) and the European Union Agency for the Space Programme (EUSPA). The constellation, comprising 290 satellites in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) and Medium Earth Orbit (MEO), will serve governmental applications such as border and maritime surveillance, crisis management, and secure communications for EU institutions, while also enabling commercial services across multiple sectors. 


The IRIS² constellation will comprise 290 satellites in LEO and MEO, providing secure communications across Europe by 2030. Credit: SES. 

 
The integration of quantum security technologies into IRIS² addresses several critical challenges. Satellites and their ground segments are increasingly a target for cyberattacks, with the number of cases on the rise since the 2000s. Quantum security technologies provide robust protection against both current and future threats, ensuring that sensitive government communications and critical infrastructure remain secure even in a post-quantum computing world. 
By incorporating quantum-resistant technologies, the constellation will provide a future-proof foundation for Europe's secure communications needs. 
 
Quantum key distribution via satellite 
One of the most promising quantum security technologies considered for future integration into IRIS² is satellite-based quantum key distribution (QKD). This emerging technology uses the laws of quantum mechanics to share encryption keys between parties, with any attempt to intercept the transmission detectable due to the quantum no-cloning theorem and the observer effect. 
The constellation's multi-orbit architecture provides particular advantages for quantum communications. In quantum key distribution, encryption keys are carried by individual photons, or particles of light, which can be lost if they travel too far through space and atmosphere. LEO satellites, being closer to Earth, provide lower latency and reduced photon loss, while MEO satellites provide broader coverage and longer contact times with ground stations. This hybrid approach ensures that quantum keys can be distributed across Europe as well as to partner regions with improved reliability and coverage. 
 
Post-quantum cryptography implementation 
In addition to QKD technologies, post-quantum cryptography (PQC) provides a complementary approach to quantum-resistant security. PQC algorithms, based on mathematical problems believed to be difficult even for quantum computers, could provide multiple layers of security with a hybrid combination of QKD and PQC possible in systems such as IRIS², further enhancing security. 
This implementation of PQC in IRIS² supports broader European cybersecurity objectives. In June 2025, the European Commission, alongside its Member States, issued a Roadmap for the Transition to Post-Quantum Cryptography, with Member States set to start transitioning to PQC by the end of 2026. 
 
Securing critical infrastructure and government communications 
The quantum security capabilities of IRIS² would directly support Europe's strategic autonomy and resilience objectives. Government communications, such as diplomatic exchanges and security coordination, require the highest levels of security. Traditional encryption methods, while currently secure, will become vulnerable as quantum computing capabilities advance. 


Leaders from the signature event of the IRIS² kick-off, in Brussels, Belgium, December 2024. From left to right: Adel al Saleh, CEO of SES, Timo Pesonen, Director-General DG DEFIS, European Commission, Eva Berneke, (former) CEO of the Eutelsat (formerly Eutelsat Group), Andrius Kublius, Commissioner for Defence and Space, Josef Aschbacher, Director General of the European Space Agency, Laurent Jaffart, Director for Connectivity and Secure Communications Directorate of the European Space Agency, Miguel Ángel Panduro, CEO of Hispasat. Credit: ESA – P. Sebirot. 

 
Quantum security technologies provide future-proof protection for these critical communications. Government users will benefit from quantum-secured channels for sensitive data transmission, secure authentication protocols that resist quantum attacks, and resilient communication networks that maintain security even under sophisticated cyber threats. 
 
Commercial applications and innovation 
Beyond governmental and critical infrastructure applications, IRIS² quantum security technologies will drive innovation in the commercial sector. The European Commission’s Network and Information Systems (NIS)2 Directive broadened the scope of sectors required to strengthen their cybersecurity capabilities. Space is now explicitly included, alongside central and regional public administrations and critical product manufacturing. These domains will benefit from quantum-secured communications. 


In January 2025, an IRIS² Industry InfoDay was hosted in Brussels, Belgium, and brought together industry leaders, innovators, subcontractors, and potential partners to help shape the future of Europe’s sovereign satellite connectivity. Credit: Hispasat. 

 
This dual-use approach, serving both governmental and commercial needs, ensures that IRIS² quantum security investments will maintain the security standards required for critical applications and sectors across Member States. 
 
European quantum technology leadership 
The potential quantum security capabilities of IRIS² reflect broader European leadership in quantum technologies. The 10-year EU Quantum Flagship initiative, launched in 2018, is investing €1 billion in quantum research across Member States. IRIS² represents a practical application of the results of these research investments, demonstrating Europe's ability to translate quantum science into operational capabilities. 
The constellation also supports the development of a European quantum technology industrial base. By creating demand for quantum communication systems and solutions, IRIS² encourages European companies to develop these critical technologies, reducing dependence on third country suppliers for security-critical systems. 
 
Challenges and future developments 
The integration of quantum security technologies into IRIS² is not without challenges. Quantum communication systems require precise pointing and mitigation capabilities to maintain quantum state fidelity over long distances. In the space environment, radiation and temperature extremes pose additional challenges for these mitigation techniques to maintain quantum system performance. 
Future developments may expand IRIS² quantum capabilities further, such as quantum computing capabilities being integrated into satellites, potentially providing distributed quantum processing power for complex applications and a comprehensive quantum-secure communication network. Europe is already developing its technical expertise, with IRIS² set to build upon proof-of-concept missions. 
 
Preparing for the quantum future 
As IRIS² moves from development to deployment, the constellation will be able to demonstrate that quantum technologies, once confined to research laboratories, are ready for operational deployment in critical infrastructure.  
The Eagle-1 satellite, the first European in-orbit demonstrator of a QKD system, is currently being developed by a SES-led industrial consortium of 20 European companies, funded by the European Commission and implemented with ESA. Scheduled for launch in 2026, Eagle-1 will demonstrate and validate QKD technologies from LEO, delivering mission data for the development and deployment of EuroQCI, before being integrated into IRIS². 


Eagle-1, Europe's first satellite QKD system, will demonstrate quantum-secured communications across European ground stations, paving the way for future quantum security implementations in IRIS². Credit: European Space Agency & SES.

 
The integration of quantum security into IRIS², with EuroQCI envisioned to become an integral part of the constellation, positions Europe at the forefront of secure space communications. 
 
Looking ahead 
IRIS² represents more than just Europe's next-generation satellite constellation. It embodies the Union's commitment to maintaining technological sovereignty and security leadership in an increasingly complex threat environment. 
The constellation's quantum capabilities will serve multiple strategic objectives: protecting critical government and infrastructure communications, supporting commercial innovation, and demonstrating European leadership in quantum technologies. With governmental services due to be fully operational by 2030, IRIS² is set to provide the secure foundation necessary for Europe's digital transformation and continued prosperity and resilience in the quantum age. 

Thu, 16/04/2026 - 12:00
articles.content:
OBSERVER: A quantum secure future with IRIS²

Thu, 16/04/2026 - 12:00

As cyber threats evolve and quantum computing advances towards practical applications, traditional encryption methods face more complex challenges. The Infrastructure for Resilience, Interconnectivity and Security by Satellite (IRIS²) constellation represents Europe's ambitious response to this emerging reality, positioning the European Union to integrate cutting-edge quantum security technologies to ensuring secure communications across Europe and Africa . IRIS² will become a multi-orbit satellite constellation for governmental and commercial communications, with an architecture creating opportunities for future quantum security implementations including quantum key distribution and post-quantum cryptography. In this Observer, we explore how quantum technologies could transform satellite communications security, examine the potential for quantum capabilities within IRIS², and assess what this means for Europe's digital sovereignty in the quantum age.

Quantum computers differ from today’s classical machines. Instead of processing information in binary, as 0s or 1s, they use quantum bits, or qubits, which can exist in overlapping states and be linked together so their states influence one another. This gives quantum computers the ability to explore many possible solutions to problems in parallel, enabling them to run special algorithms designed for quantum systems to solve the mathematical problems underlying many current encryption methods. The potential ease and speed with which quantum computers could crack encryption poses a fundamental threat to cybersecurity infrastructure. Quantum computers already exist as experimental systems, but once cryptanalytically relevant machines become available, they will be capable of breaking the mathematical foundations underlying today’s encryption that protects everything from online banking to government communications.

This looming threat, often referred to as Years to Quantum or “Y2Q” , has prompted governments and organisations worldwide to prepare for a post-quantum world. In order to anticipate risks and exploit growing opportunities, the European Commission announced its Quantum Europe Strategy in July 2025, recognising this challenge as a matter of strategic importance. The strategy marked Space and Dual-Use ( the capacity of quantum technologies to serve both civilian and security/defence ends ) technologies as a crucial area, in which IRIS² and the European Quantum Communication Infrastructure (EuroQCI) initiative present tremendous opportunities to leverage quantum technologies in the space domain.

IRIS² and the quantum security challenge

IRIS², Europe's sovereign multi-orbit constellation initiative, is being designed with quantum-ready security in mind. The European Union’s newest flagship space component, is led by the European Commission under a concession contract with the SpaceRISE consortium , working with trusted partners such as the European Space Agency (ESA) and the European Union Agency for the Space Programme (EUSPA) . The constellation, comprising 290 satellites in Low Earth Orbit (LEO) and Medium Earth Orbit (MEO), will serve governmental applications such as border and maritime surveillance, crisis management, and secure communications for EU institutions, while also enabling commercial services across multiple sectors.

The IRIS² constellation will comprise 290 satellites in LEO and MEO, providing secure communications across Europe by 2030. Credit: SES.

The integration of quantum security technologies into IRIS² addresses several critical challenges. Satellites and their ground segments are increasingly a target for cyberattacks, with the number of cases on the rise since the 2000s . Quantum security technologies provide robust protection against both current and future threats, ensuring that sensitive government communications and critical infrastructure remain secure even in a post-quantum computing world.

By incorporating quantum-resistant technologies, the constellation will provide a future-proof foundation for Europe's secure communications needs.

Quantum key distribution via satellite

One of the most promising quantum security technologies considered for future integration into IRIS² is satellite-based quantum key distribution (QKD). This emerging technology uses the laws of quantum mechanics to share encryption keys between parties, with any attempt to intercept the transmission detectable due to the quantum no-cloning theorem and the observer effect.

The constellation's multi-orbit architecture provides particular advantages for quantum communications. In quantum key distribution, encryption keys are carried by individual photons, or particles of light, which can be lost if they travel too far through space and atmosphere. LEO satellites, being closer to Earth, provide lower latency and reduced photon loss, while MEO satellites provide broader coverage and longer contact times with ground stations. This hybrid approach ensures that quantum keys can be distributed across Europe as well as to partner regions with improved reliability and coverage.

Post-quantum cryptography implementation

In addition to QKD technologies, post-quantum cryptography (PQC) provides a complementary approach to quantum-resistant security. PQC algorithms, based on mathematical problems believed to be difficult even for quantum computers, could provide multiple layers of security with a hybrid combination of QKD and PQC possible in systems such as IRIS², further enhancing security.

This implementation of PQC in IRIS² supports broader European cybersecurity objectives. In June 2025, the European Commission, alongside its Member States, issued a Roadmap for the Transition to Post-Quantum Cryptography , with Member States set to start transitioning to PQC by the end of 2026.

Securing critical infrastructure and government communications

The quantum security capabilities of IRIS² would directly support Europe's strategic autonomy and resilience objectives. Government communications, such as diplomatic exchanges and security coordination, require the highest levels of security. Traditional encryption methods, while currently secure, will become vulnerable as quantum computing capabilities advance.

Leaders from the signature event of the IRIS² kick-off, in Brussels, Belgium, December 2024. From left to right: Adel al Saleh, CEO of SES, Timo Pesonen, Director-General DG DEFIS, European Commission, Eva Berneke, (former) CEO of the Eutelsat (formerly Eutelsat Group), Andrius Kublius, Commissioner for Defence and Space, Josef Aschbacher, Director General of the European Space Agency, Laurent Jaffart, Director for Connectivity and Secure Communications Directorate of the European Space Agency, Miguel Ángel Panduro, CEO of Hispasat. Credit: ESA – P. Sebirot.

Quantum security technologies provide future-proof protection for these critical communications. Government users will benefit from quantum-secured channels for sensitive data transmission, secure authentication protocols that resist quantum attacks, and resilient communication networks that maintain security even under sophisticated cyber threats.

Commercial applications and innovation

Beyond governmental and critical infrastructure applications, IRIS² quantum security technologies will drive innovation in the commercial sector. The European Commission’s Network and Information Systems (NIS)2 Directive broadened the scope of sectors required to strengthen their cybersecurity capabilities. Space is now explicitly included, alongside central and regional public administrations and critical product manufacturing. These domains will benefit from quantum-secured communications.

In January 2025, an IRIS² Industry InfoDay was hosted in Brussels, Belgium, and brought together industry leaders, innovators, subcontractors, and potential partners to help shape the future of Europe’s sovereign satellite connectivity. Credit: Hispasat.

This dual-use approach, serving both governmental and commercial needs, ensures that IRIS² quantum security investments will maintain the security standards required for critical applications and sectors across Member States.

European quantum technology leadership

The potential quantum security capabilities of IRIS² reflect broader European leadership in quantum technologies. The 10-year EU Quantum Flagship initiative, launched in 2018, is investing €1 billion in quantum research across Member States. IRIS² represents a practical application of the results of these research investments, demonstrating Europe's ability to translate quantum science into operational capabilities.

The constellation also supports the development of a European quantum technology industrial base. By creating demand for quantum communication systems and solutions, IRIS² encourages European companies to develop these critical technologies, reducing dependence on third country suppliers for security-critical systems.

Challenges and future developments

The integration of quantum security technologies into IRIS² is not without challenges. Quantum communication systems require precise pointing and mitigation capabilities to maintain quantum state fidelity over long distances . In the space environment, radiation and temperature extremes pose additional challenges for these mitigation techniques to maintain quantum system performance.

Future developments may expand IRIS² quantum capabilities further, such as quantum computing capabilities being integrated into satellites , potentially providing distributed quantum processing power for complex applications and a comprehensive quantum-secure communication network. Europe is already developing its technical expertise, with IRIS² set to build upon proof-of-concept missions.

Preparing for the quantum future

As IRIS² moves from development to deployment, the constellation will be able to demonstrate that quantum technologies, once confined to research laboratories, are ready for operational deployment in critical infrastructure.

The Eagle-1 satellite , the first European in-orbit demonstrator of a QKD system, is currently being developed by a SES-led industrial consortium of 20 European companies, funded by the European Commission and implemented with ESA. Scheduled for launch in 2026, Eagle-1 will demonstrate and validate QKD technologies from LEO, delivering mission data for the development and deployment of EuroQCI, before being integrated into IRIS² .

Eagle-1, Europe's first satellite QKD system, will demonstrate quantum-secured communications across European ground stations, paving the way for future quantum security implementations in IRIS². Credit: European Space Agency & SES.

The integration of quantum security into IRIS², with EuroQCI envisioned to become an integral part of the constellation, positions Europe at the forefront of secure space communications.

Looking ahead

IRIS² represents more than just Europe's next-generation satellite constellation. It embodies the Union's commitment to maintaining technological sovereignty and security leadership in an increasingly complex threat environment.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the IRIS² satellite constellation initiative integrating advanced quantum security technologies to protect governmental and commercial communications across Europe and Africa. This represents a significant technological breakthrough with broad societal impact by future-proofing cybersecurity infrastructure against emerging quantum computing threats. The story is detailed, focused on a single major development, and highlights Europe's leadership and strategic autonomy in quantum-secure communications, benefiting a wide range of sectors and the general public.
articles.personality_title: Europe advances secure communications with quantum-ready IRIS² satellite network
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – As quantum computers develop, they threaten to break today’s encryption methods, risking the security of online banking, government messages, and more. To prepare, the European Union is building a new satellite network called IRIS², designed to protect communications using advanced quantum security technologies.

**What happened** – IRIS² is a constellation of 290 satellites planned to orbit Earth by 2030. It will provide secure connections for governments and businesses across Europe and Africa. The satellites use two key quantum technologies: quantum key distribution (QKD), which shares encryption keys using particles of light that cannot be intercepted without detection, and post-quantum cryptography (PQC), which uses math that even quantum computers find hard to solve. The first test satellite, Eagle-1, will launch in 2026 to demonstrate these quantum techniques.

**Impact** – IRIS² will help protect sensitive communications against future cyberattacks, even when powerful quantum computers become common. It offers a strong shield for government data, border surveillance, and emergency responses, while also benefiting commercial sectors that need secure space-based communication. This project supports Europe’s goal to lead in quantum technology and reduce reliance on other countries for critical security systems.

**What's next step** – After Eagle-1’s launch and testing, IRIS² will continue integrating quantum security into its full satellite network. The European Commission and member states will also begin switching to post-quantum cryptography on the ground by 2026. Over time, IRIS² might add quantum computing capabilities in space, expanding its security and processing power.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Europe’s IRIS² satellite constellation is pioneering quantum security to protect communications now and in the future, ensuring safer connections across continents as quantum computing advances.
articles.personality_title_fr: L'Europe sécurise ses communications avec le réseau satellitaire IRIS² prêt pour le quantique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Avec le développement des ordinateurs quantiques, les méthodes actuelles de cryptage risquent d’être cassées, mettant en danger la sécurité des banques en ligne, des communications gouvernementales, et plus. Pour se préparer, l’Union européenne construit un nouveau réseau de satellites appelé IRIS², conçu pour protéger les communications grâce à des technologies quantiques avancées.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – IRIS² est une constellation de 290 satellites prévue pour orbiter autour de la Terre d’ici 2030. Elle fournira des connexions sécurisées pour les gouvernements et les entreprises en Europe et en Afrique. Les satellites utilisent deux technologies quantiques clés : la distribution quantique de clés (QKD), qui partage les clés de cryptage via des particules de lumière impossibles à intercepter sans être détectées, et la cryptographie post-quantique (PQC), qui utilise des mathématiques difficiles même pour les ordinateurs quantiques. Le premier satellite test, Eagle-1, sera lancé en 2026 pour démontrer ces techniques.

**Impact** – IRIS² aidera à protéger les communications sensibles contre les cyberattaques futures, même lorsque les ordinateurs quantiques puissants seront courants. Il offrira une protection solide pour les données gouvernementales, la surveillance des frontières et la gestion des crises, tout en bénéficiant aux secteurs commerciaux ayant besoin de communications spatiales sécurisées. Ce projet soutient l’objectif de l’Europe de devenir leader en technologies quantiques et de réduire sa dépendance envers d’autres pays pour ses systèmes de sécurité critiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Après le lancement et les tests d’Eagle-1, IRIS² continuera d’intégrer la sécurité quantique dans son réseau complet de satellites. La Commission européenne et les États membres commenceront aussi à adopter la cryptographie post-quantique au sol dès 2026. À terme, IRIS² pourrait ajouter des capacités de calcul quantique dans l’espace, augmentant sa sécurité et sa puissance de traitement.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La constellation satellitaire IRIS² de l’Europe innove avec la sécurité quantique pour protéger les communications aujourd’hui et demain, assurant des connexions plus sûres à travers les continents face aux progrès du calcul quantique.
articles.personality_title_es: Europa avanza en comunicaciones seguras con la red satelital IRIS² lista para la era cuántica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – A medida que los ordenadores cuánticos avanzan, amenazan con romper los métodos actuales de cifrado, poniendo en riesgo la seguridad de la banca en línea, mensajes gubernamentales y más. Para prepararse, la Unión Europea está construyendo una nueva red de satélites llamada IRIS², diseñada para proteger las comunicaciones usando tecnologías cuánticas avanzadas.

**Qué pasó** – IRIS² es una constelación de 290 satélites que se lanzarán para orbitar la Tierra para 2030. Proporcionará conexiones seguras para gobiernos y empresas en Europa y África. Los satélites usan dos tecnologías cuánticas clave: distribución cuántica de claves (QKD), que comparte claves de cifrado usando partículas de luz que no se pueden interceptar sin ser detectadas, y criptografía post-cuántica (PQC), que usa matemáticas difíciles incluso para ordenadores cuánticos. El primer satélite de prueba, Eagle-1, se lanzará en 2026 para demostrar estas técnicas.

**Impacto** – IRIS² ayudará a proteger las comunicaciones sensibles contra futuros ciberataques, incluso cuando los potentes ordenadores cuánticos sean comunes. Ofrece una fuerte protección para datos gubernamentales, vigilancia fronteriza y respuestas a emergencias, beneficiando también a sectores comerciales que necesitan comunicación segura desde el espacio. Este proyecto apoya el objetivo de Europa de liderar en tecnología cuántica y reducir dependencia de otros países para sistemas críticos de seguridad.

**Próximo paso** – Tras el lanzamiento y pruebas de Eagle-1, IRIS² seguirá integrando seguridad cuántica en toda su red satelital. La Comisión Europea y los estados miembros también comenzarán a adoptar criptografía post-cuántica en tierra para 2026. Con el tiempo, IRIS² podría añadir capacidades de computación cuántica en el espacio, ampliando su seguridad y potencia de procesamiento.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La constelación satelital IRIS² de Europa lidera la seguridad cuántica para proteger las comunicaciones ahora y en el futuro, asegurando conexiones más seguras entre continentes frente a los avances en computación cuántica.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a constellation of stylized satellites orbiting Earth, connected by glowing quantum light beams symbolizing secure quantum key distribution, with abstract representations of intertwined quantum bits (qubits) floating between the satellites and a softly illuminated European continent below, all rendered in natural, simple colors and gentle brushstrokes conveying innovation and protection.
